ocr.text: The following data table presents an analysis of the relationship between drug use in combination and various genetic traits.
This investigation primarily focuses on the Variable of interest, which encompasses distinct combinations of substances, and
their corresponding Adjusted Odds Ratios (AOR). The AOR serves as a critical metric in understanding the likelihood of
experiencing specific outcomes associated with these combinations, as indicated by the values presented in the table.
Variable  Adjusted Odds Ratio (AOR)  95% Confidence Interval (CI)  p-value
Ever used drugs in combination
Ever had drugs planted by police
Median daily expenses for purchasing drugs
Furthermore, the inclusion of the 95% Confidence Interval (CI) allows for a robust assessment of the precision of these
estimates, providing insight into the reliability of the observed associations.
QTL  Trait(s)  Chromosome  Start (Mb)  Max (Mb) and Marker  Stop (Mb)  Percent Variation Explained (%)
HrS6  D3% weight  Chr 9  116.476207  121.771517 backupJAX00708075  Telomere  7
HrS9  Hemorrhage  Chr 15  Centromere  30.785867 UNC150077326  64.430001  9.10
The analysis also highlights significant findings related to specific chromosomes, including Chromosome 18, which is
associated with the trait HrS5. Notably, this chromosome spans a region from 27.108062 Mb to its maximum marker. The
Percent Variation Explained (%) is another essential metric, elucidating the extent to which genetic factors contribute to the
observed variation in drug response. Overall, this table underscores the intricate interplay between genetic markers and the
effects of drug use in combination, paving the way for further investigations into their implications for treatment and prevention
strategies.
